architecture.summary: Self-Refine is a test-time iterative refinement loop that uses a single LLM as generator, feedback provider, and refiner. The system generates an initial output via few-shot prompting, then feeds that output back to the same LLM (with a task-specific feedback prompt) to generate natural-language critiques. The LLM then uses its own feedback plus the history of previous iterations to produce a refined output. This feedback-refine cycle repeats for up to 4 iterations or until a task-specific stopping condition is met. No training, RL, or external models are required—only prompts and the base LLM's in-context learning capacity.

claims.dataset: aggregated across 7 diverse tasks (Dialogue Response Generation, Code Optimization, Code Readability Improvement, Math Reasoning, Sentiment Reversal, Acronym Generation, Constrained Generation)
claims.statement: Across all evaluated tasks, outputs generated with Self-Refine are preferred by humans and automatic metrics over those generated with the same LLM using conventional one-step generation, improving by ~20% absolute on average in task performance.
GPT-4 + Self-Refine improves over base GPT-4 by 8.7% (absolute) in Code Optimization, increasing optimization percentage from 27.3% to 36.0%.
In Dialogue Response Generation, GPT-4 preference score improves by 49.2% – from 25.4% to 74.6%.
Self-Refine improves the initial generation by up to absolute 13% when applied to strong code models such as Codex (code-davinci-002) in code-generation tasks.
Self-Refine does not require any supervised training data, additional training, or reinforcement learning, and instead uses a single LLM as the generator, refiner and the feedback provider.

Feedback quality plays a crucial role in Self-Refine. Specific, actionable feedback yields superior results compared to generic feedback or no feedback. In sentiment transfer, changing from actionable feedback to generic feedback leads to a significant performance drop (43.2 to 31.2), and the task fails without feedback.

The modest performance gains in Math Reasoning can be traced back to the inability to accurately identify whether there is any error. In math, errors can be nuanced and sometimes limited to a single line or incorrect operation. A consistent-looking reasoning chain can deceive LLMs to think that everything looks good (e.g., ChatGPT feedback for 94% instances is 'everything looks good').

Self-Refine does not work consistently with weaker models. When instantiated with Vicuna-13B, it struggled significantly with the refinement process—the model was not able to consistently generate feedback in the required format and often failed to adhere to refinement prompts, either repeating the same output or generating hallucinated content.
